coredata.dc:title: Manganese oxide-carbon quantum dots nano-composites for fluorescence/magnetic resonance (T1) dual mode bioimaging, long term cell tracking, and ROS scavenging
coredata.dc:creator: Das, Bodhisatwa
Girigoswami, Agnishwar
Pal, Pallabi
Dhara, Santanu
coredata.dc:description: Multimodal long-term imaging probes with capability of extracting complementary information are highly important in biomedical engineering for disease diagnosis and monitoring of therapeutics distribution. However, most of the theranostics probes used are transient and have inherent problem of toxicity mostly related to generation of free radicals. In current study, a simple microwave assisted synthesis of multimodal imaging nanoprobe (T1 contrast in MR/fluorescence) is reported via doping carbon quantum dots into manganese oxide nanoparticles. The nanostructures were characterized by US-Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, TEM, XRD, AFM and XPS. The average particle size was observed to be around 20–40 nm with a height of 7–9 nm and approximate quantum yield of 0.23. The nanostructures were useful for bio imaging and cell tracking via fluorescence microscopy up to 12 generations with nominal cytotoxicity. The material was capable of scavenging free radicals from cellular microenvironment and downregulate gene expression of free radical scavenging enzymes. The material has significant relaxivity (r1) value of 3.98 mM−1.sec−1 at 1.5 T. It was also observed to create significant contrast with high circulation time (30 min) and renal clearance property. The histological analysis of kidney and liver sections were observed to have no significant toxicity from the nanostructure.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of manganese oxide nanoparticles doped CDs (MnCs)
In vitro cell culture and cell tracking study
Histological analysis of liver and kidney
In vitro bioimaging, cytotoxicity, and cell tracking study
DNA quantification and in vitro ROS scavenging activity

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge MHRD and CSIR (Govt of India) for the fellowship of Bodhisatwa Das and Pallabi Pal respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different spectroscopic analysis of MnC a) UV–Vis spectroscopy; b) fluorescence spectroscopy c) FTIR spectra d) Raman spectroscopy.
Analysis of MnC structure by a) TEM bright field imaging, b) control image of CDs, c) SAED analysis, d) magnified image of MnCs showing deposition of CDs, e) XRD analysis, f) AFM image.
XPS analysis of MnCs a) total scan, b) C1s, c) Mn2p/3, d) O1s.
Bioimaging of MSCs using a) MnCs; b) DAPI; c) merged; d) MTT assay results (image captured at 20× magnification; * implies p ≤ 0.05 considered as statistically significant).
Cell tracking study using MnCs a) day 0, b) day 3, c) day 5, d) day 7, e) day 9 via fluorescence microscopy (fluorescence and DIC image merged; scale 50 μm).
a) DNA quantification; b) DCFH-DA assay; c) NBT assay (* implies p ≤ 0.05 considered as statistically significant).
a) Hemolysis assay; b) RBC aggregation study; c) WBC aggregation study; d) platelet aggregation study for MnCs (images captured at 10× magnification).

RT-PCR study of cells post application of MnCs and UV exposure (* denotes p ≤ 0.05 to be considered as statistically significant; genes explored are GAPDH as housekeeping gene, glutathione transferase, NAD-quinone-oxidoreductase and superoxide dismutase respectively).
MR Imaging of MnCs a) concentration-dependent T1 phantom imaging, b) concentration versus r1 plot for the determination of relaxivity.
MR image a) before injection, b) immediately after injection (10 min) c) 30 min after injection (arrows depict enhancement in contrast).
Histological images (H&E) of sections of a) kidney and b) liver (inset higher magnification images).
Proposed mechanism of ROS scavenging by CD doping.
List of primer sequences and respective melting temperature.

head.ce:title: Manganese oxide-carbon quantum dots nano-composites for fluorescence/magnetic resonance (T1) dual mode bioimaging, long term cell tracking, and ROS scavenging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, WB, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Allied Health Sciences, Chettinad Hospital & Research Institute (CHRI), Chettinad Academy of Research & Education (CARE), Kelambakkam, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multimodal long-term imaging probes with capability of extracting complementary information are highly important in biomedical engineering for disease diagnosis and monitoring of therapeutics distribution. However, most of the theranostics probes used are transient and have inherent problem of toxicity mostly related to generation of free radicals. In current study, a simple microwave assisted synthesis of multimodal imaging nanoprobe (T1 contrast in MR/fluorescence) is reported via doping carbon quantum dots into manganese oxide nanoparticles. The nanostructures were characterized by US-Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, Raman spectroscopy, TEM, XRD, AFM and XPS. The average particle size was observed to be around 20–40 nm with a height of 7–9 nm and approximate quantum yield of 0.23. The nanostructures were useful for bio imaging and cell tracking via fluorescence microscopy up to 12 generations with nominal cytotoxicity. The material was capable of scavenging free radicals from cellular microenvironment and downregulate gene expression of free radical scavenging enzymes. The material has significant relaxivity (r1) value of 3.98 mM−1.sec−1 at 1.5 T. It was also observed to create significant contrast with high circulation time (30 min) and renal clearance property. The histological analysis of kidney and liver sections were observed to have no significant toxicity from the nanostructure.

ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is one of the major diagnostic tools in current medical practices. It is highly effective in obtaining high spatial resolution and soft tissue contrast in complete non-invasive means. Even MR imaging is much safer with respect to other tomographic techniques as no radioisotope or ionization radiation is utilized [1–3]. This is useful for neural imaging, cardiac diagnostics, and cancer diagnostics because of its significant soft tissue imaging property. However, low intensity of the signal and poor signal to noise ratio are major limitations of MRI which can lead to the wrong diagnosis. To enhance the signal quality, different contrast agents are utilized in MRI diagnosis [4]. The major contrast agent used to enhance T1 i.e. longitudinal relaxivity (r1) acceleration are paramagnetic Gadolinium (Gd) based chelates. These materials enhance the contrast between the required organ and its surrounding by brightening the image [5,6].

Even though given as a coordination compound, Gd-based contrast agents are significantly toxic. It is known to provide hepatotoxicity and nephritic fibrosis [7]. In vitro models suggest the development of significant levels reactive oxygen species and teratogenicity in Gd-based nanoparticles [8,9]. These limitations allow researchers to think of other T1 contrast agents for better biocompatibility. Manganese oxide-based materials are some of the major materials useful as a contrast agent. They are useful for significantly high-water proton relaxivity. Manganese and Mn-oxide based nanoparticles have been observed significantly effective in imaging gastro-intestinal-tract lesions and reticular endothelial system [10,11]. Due to higher biocompatibility, this material is observed to be useful for cell labeling and tracking [12].

Multimodalities in imaging system allow generation of novel sets of data due to correlation and imaging. Therefore, often material scientists are interested in developing multimodal imaging probe. Along with multimodality, the researchers are often also interested in their theranostics potentials (therapeutic and imaging together). Manganese oxide nanoparticles are often accompanied by drug delivery properties [13,14]. It is also accompanied with multiple imaging modalities such as fluorescence, PET etc. [15,16]

Even though manganese-based probes are much safer with respect to a Gd-based T1 contrast agent, there is evidence of ROS induced toxicity into in vivo models [17]. Therefore, there should be inherent mechanism into the nanoprobes which can curb ROS-induced cytotoxicity. In the generation of smart nanoprobes, it should be multitasking and multimodal. Fluorescence based bioimaging is complementary in terms of features in imaging modality. While MRI is useful in detailed imaging of intricate anatomical details in 3D like distribution of blood vessels. Fluorescence in the other hand is useful for targeted imaging of individual cellular level, biomolecule imaging and functional imaging at the microscopic level [18]. In very recent reports we have found that combination of fluorescent nanoprobe with MRI contrast agent is highly useful for functional imaging and distribution tracking of cells and biomolecules in vivo [19].

In recent studies, it has been shown that carbon nanodots (CDs) are fluorescent nanoprobe with in situ ROS scavenging property [20]. Also, manganese oxide nanoparticles supported on carbon nanoparticles are observed to be effective for oxygen reduction reactions [21]. From the above rationale, in current study CD doped manganese oxide nanoparticles (MnCs) were synthesized and explored as a multimodal bioimaging probe for fluorescence/T1 contrast imaging probe with in situ ROS scavenging property and long-term cell tracking.

All the materials utilized in the current study are used as it is without further purification if not mentioned. For all the reactions deionized water (DI water, conductivity: 18.5 mΩ cm−1) was utilized. Sucrose, Na2HPO4, KH2PO4, NaCl, KCl, NaOH, KMnO4 etc. were procured from Merck India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of manganese oxide nanoparticles doped CDs (MnCs)
ce:section.ce:para: For synthesis of MnCs, 0.5 M sucrose solution was prepared, and solution pH was raised to 11 by 1 M NaOH. It was immediately transferred to a domestic microwave (1000 W, 2.5 GHz) and irradiated for 3 min. The solution turns pale yellow from colorless. This solution was neutralized with 1 M HCl and heated at 70 °C. Acidified KMnO4 (0.1 M) solution was added to this solution dropwise in presence of vigorous stirring in 1:5 volume ratio. The solution turns yellowish brown after 2 h of stirring. The solution was filtered using 0.2 μm syringe filter and freeze dried. For further biological studies, 100 μg·mL−1 suspensions in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were utilized if not mentioned otherwise.

The synthesized nanostructures were characterized with UV–Vis spectroscopy (Varian Cary), fluorescence spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer LS55), XPS (VG scientific ESCALAB MK-2, Mg Kα), HRTEM (JEOL, JEM-2100), EDAX (Oxford Instruments), FTIR (Thermo Nicolet, Nexus 8700), Raman spectroscopy (JobinYvon Horiba, T 64000), AFM (Agilent 5500). The measurement of optical band gap energy in solution phase was measured with a “cut off” frequency at 625 nm. Fluorescence quantum yield was measured using the formula (taking quinine sulfate as standard),

Here, Qy is quantum yield. F represents the fluorescence intensity. Refractive indexes of the solvents were represented by η. A is absorbance of the suspensions. Here the sample and the references are nanostructure suspension and quinine sulfate solution of same concentration (1 mg/mL), respectively. The Qyref value was taken to be 0.54.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cell culture and cell tracking study
ce:section.ce:para: Bone marrow-derived mesenchymal stem cells (MSCs) were isolated from rat femur bone under Institute Animal Ethical Committee's permission and cultured in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM-high glucose) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic (HiMedia, India). 3-[4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay was conducted according to the protocol described by Mossman T (1983) [22]. The MTT assay was used to measure the mitochondrial activity, which is correlated to cell viability for both attached and poorly attached cells. Metabolically active cells can reduce the MTT tetrazolium salt to formazan crystals, while dead cells do not. Cells were grown on cell culture plates, with an initial seeding density of 104 cells per well, and the samples were added along with the media. The cells were incubated for 1 day, 3 days and 7 days with different concentrations of MnC (1–4 mg/mL, dissolved in cell culture media). The culture media was discarded and the MTT salt solution (Invitrogen) was added to a final concentration of 5 mg/ mL. The samples were incubated for 4 h. Finally, the cells were lysed by DMSO and the optical density was measured at 590 nm.

The bio-imaging study was conducted by growing cells on poly-l-lysine coated coverslips (Blue-Star) (104 cells/sample). The MnC mixed media was added (5 μg/mL) to the cells and incubated for 12 h. The media was removed, and the coverslips were washed with PBS. The cells were counterstained with DAPI (Life Technologies) for nucleus and imaged using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Carl Zeiss, Germany). The coverslips were mounted on glass slides (Hi Media) and imaged using both fluorescences as well as the DIC (differential interference contrast) mode. Two filters of excitation (365 nm bandpass 25 nm, and 470 nm bandpass 50 nm) and emission (450 nm bandpass 25 nm, and 540 nm bandpass 25 nm) were employed for capturing the fluorescence micrographs. All the micrographs were captured at 20× magnification. For cell tracking study MnC mixed media (5 μg/mL) was utilized for 12 h incubation of cells in the T-25 poly-l-lysine coated cell culture flasks. After 12 h the media in the flask was changed and replaced with fresh media (without MnC). The media of the flask was replaced with fresh media for every 72 h and cells were passage into 1:3 for next 9 days with imaging of the cells both in fluorescence and DIC mode directly from the flask.

Hemolysis assay was conducted in view of the material is applicable to in vivo systems, like the optical imaging of blood vessels and blood-related sensing assays. Briefly, uncoagulated blood (3% citrate) was mixed with an equal volume of sterile normal saline. Different concentrations of samples were mixed with 1 mL of saline mixed blood (volume balanced by PBS) and incubated for 60 min. Subsequently, the samples were centrifuged, and the optical densities were measured at 540 nm. Negative and positive controls were taken as normal saline and 1% Triton-X solution.

For the blood cell aggregation study, 5 mL of blood was collected from the subject (mixed with 3% Na-citrate 100 μL/mL of blood) and subdivided into two parts for RBC aggregation and for WBC and platelets adhesion studies. For the RBC aggregation assay, 2 mL blood was centrifuged at 700 rpm for 10 min. The pellet was collected, and the pellet was mixed with normal saline in 1: 9 volume ratios. From that solution, 100 μL was taken and mixed with 600 μL of normal saline in the test tubes for each sample. Rest of the volume was filled by addition of saline to make up to 1 mL. The sample was incubated for 60 min, and afterward, one drop of the resultant solution was placed on a glass slide and observed under a microscope. For platelet and WBC aggregation assays, 4 mL uncoagulated blood was mixed with an equal volume of saline. This solution was mixed with HiSep™LSM-1077 (HiMedia) in a volume ratio of 3: 4. This mixture was centrifuged at 400g for 30 min. The mixture during this treatment was separated into three distinct layers of platelet-rich plasma, WBC, and RBC. The RBC, platelet rich plasma and WBC layers were isolated and mixed with the sample in a 9:1 volume ratio. After incubation of 60 min, a drop was placed on a glass slide and observed under a microscope.

The synthesized nanostructures were explored for imaging as well as therapeutic applications. In vitro hydroxyl free radical scavenging property of the synthesized nanostructures was verified via Di-Chloro Di-Hydrofuran Fluorescein Di-Acetate (DCFH-DA) assay. Briefly, 10,000 cells per well were cultured on a poly-l-lysine-coated cell culture plate. Different concentrations of nanostructures were added to the wells. After 72 h of culture, the culture medium was discarded and washed with sterile PBS. The cells were incubated with 1 mM methanolic DCFH-DA solution (Sigma-Aldrich) for 1 h and the fluorescence intensity was measured by excitation at 485 nm and emission at 530 nm. The inhibition of hydroxyl radical was measured with respect to the controls (without samples).

NBT (Nitro Blue Tetrazolium) assays were performed in vitro both by spectroscopic and microscopic methods by using the protocol described by Choi H. et al. (2009), but with certain modifications [23]. Briefly, 6 mg/mL NBT (SRL Chemicals, Mumbai) solution was prepared in PBS and protected from light. MSCs cultured in DMEM (HiMedia) were added in poly-l-lysine coated culture plates for growth in NBT supplemented media. Different concentrations of samples were incubated with cells. Finally, the media was discarded, and the cells were treated using DMSO and 1 M KOH solution. Finally, the optical density was measured at 610 nm.

The gene expression down-regulation in presence of MnCs was studied via RT-PCR analysis. MSCs were seeded in the six-well plates and allowed to grow in presence of MnCs. RNA was isolated after 5 days of culture with MnCs and different gene expression was studied using a kit-based protocol provided by Thermo-Fischer. GAPDH was kept as house-keeping gene and its expression was checked. The oxidative stress marker gene expression down-regulation in presence of MnCs was studied via RT-PCR analysis. MSCs were seeded in the six-well plates and allowed to grow in cell culture media in presence of MnCs. RNA was isolated after 15d of culture and two consecutive days of UV exposure for 1 h and concentration was balanced via measuring it in “Nanodrop” (Biorad). Different gene expression was studied as per the protocol given in manufacturer defined manual of PCR kit (Genetix). The different gene expressions were considered in this study is glutathione-transferase (GT), NAD-quinone oxidoreductase (NAD), superoxide dismutase (SOD), and GAPDH (housekeeping gene). For MnCs, mostly the ROS scavenging enzyme gene expressions of the cells were examined to check the level of oxidative stress and photo toxicity (primer sequences and melting temperature provided in Table 1 ).

The phantom MR imaging of MnC was performed with water dispersed solution in 24 well plates using GE signaHDxT1.5 T MRI scanner (20–120 μg·mL−1). The Longitudinal relaxation times (T1) were determined based on the inversion recovery spin-echo sequences with 9 variable inversion times (TI) ranging from 400 ms to 2000 ms keeping repetition time (TR) at 3000 ms and echo time (TE) at 14 ms. The acquired images had 24 mm × 24 mm FOV and 2 mm slice thickness and the T1 values for each concentration of particle were calculated using the formula S
TI
=
S 0
×
1 − 2
e
−
TI
T 1
. The resulting longitudinal relaxivity (1/T1) values were then plotted against the concentration to determine the average relaxivity of MnC.

Animal experiments were executed following the protocol approved by CPCSEA affiliated Institutional Animal Ethics Committee (IAEC) of Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur. Healthy Swiss albino mice weighing 25–35 g were chosen for the T1 weighted MR imaging. Coronal T1 sections were collected before, immediately after intravenous injection of MnC (dosage of 2 mg/Kg. body weight) and 0.5 h postinjection maintaining the parameters TR = 250 ms, TE = 20 ms, slice thickness = 2 mm and 256 × 256 matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Histological analysis of liver and kidney
ce:section.ce:para: The animals were sacrificed after the MR imaging and liver and kidney was taken for preparing wax block to pursue histology. The tissues were crosslinked using 4% paraformaldehyde and further dehydrated (alcohol gradient) to prepared blocks. Approximately 5 μm thickness slices were prepared for histological staining. The de-waxing was performed by xylene followed by alcoholic rehydration. The slides were stained withHematoxylin-Eosin staining protocol. The nuclei were stained with hematoxylin (3–4 min) and rinsed in running tap water. Sections were further stained with eosin for 1 min followed by washing water to remove excess dye. Finally, the slides were imaged under an inverted microscope attached with a color camera (Carl Zeiss).
All the samples utilized were with a minimum sample size of (n = 3) until mentioned and Student's t-test was conducted to consider statistical significance up to p ≤ 0.05.

The physicochemical characterizations of the samples were conducted to check its property and probable applications. The UV–Vis spectrum did not show any specific signature except a typical notch around 350 nm (Fig. 1a). The “cutoff wavelength was observed to be around 625 nm and the optical band gap was observed to be around 1.98 eV. The fluorescent spectra were observed to be regular, and leptokurtic in nature (Fig. 1b). It was observed to have excitation maxima around 400 nm while the emission maximum was observed to be around 500 nm. The excitation plot was narrow while the emission band was observed to be broader in nature decaying till 700 nm. The QY of the MnCs with respect to Quinine Sulfate was observed to be around 0.23.

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1c) was observed to show different features like CH bend (860 cm−1), CO linkages (1100–1300 cm−1), C-C/C=C linkages (1500–1650 cm−1), NH (2400 cm-1), CC (2200 cm−1), COO- (3000 cm−1). Therefore, the MnCs are rich in surface active groups for bioconjugation. From Raman spectroscopy results it can be concluded that there is the presence of G and D band at 1200 and 1600 cm−1 which depict graphitic carbon structure and classic signatures of formation of CDs (Fig. 1d).

TEM results revealed that MnCs are basically irregular flake shaped nanoparticles with average size of 20–40 nm (Fig. 2a). The CDs are densely populated over MO structures as tiny dots of 1–2 nm size (Fig. 2d) as shown by red arrows. This matches with the control CD morphology (Fig. 2b). SAED pattern was reported to be like some spots upon rings which prove the presence of nano-crystalline phase (Fig. 2c). The spots obtained matches with Mn5O8 molecules (JCPDS file number 18-0801).

The XRD analysis had a similar trend as SAED pattern (Fig. 2e). The XRD had mostly the angles of JCPDS file 18-0801 like 21.71°, 28.75°, 31.28°, 31.97°, 35.92°, 47.9°, 78.7° etc. There were peaks of C atoms like 42.19, 52.31 (C8) and 46.2 and 27.8 (graphite) also (XRD peaks are marked for specific phases in the figure) Even though there was no trace of carbon like patterns in SAED. This could be also attributed to a mixture of crystalline and amorphous phases of carbon present in the structure. The AFM analysis showed the height distribution of the MnCs in the order of 3–9 nm with sharp peaks at the tip region (Fig. 2f).

The chemical analysis of the sample was conducted by XPS spectral analysis (Fig. 3a). In the XPS analysis of C1S spectrum, we find peaks near 283 eV which is mostly attributed to tetravalent metal and carbon bonding and can be attributed to Mn-Carbide like bonds. However, a sharp peak at 285 eV is also observed attributing to C1S. A broad peak from 284 to 287 eV was also observed which could be attributed to CO linkages in general. In the spectra of Mn2p/3, we mostly find peaks for metallic Mn (638.7 eV) and Mn oxides (641 eV for MnO and 641.6 eV for MnO2). A sharp peak around 652 eV was also observed which could be attributed higher Mn oxides which match with TEM and XRD results. In O1s spectra, peaks for MnO (530 eV), and CO (531 and 532 eV) were observed. From FTIR and XPS it can be also postulated that the linkage between carbon and manganese nanostructures might have through Mn-O-C linkages as well as direct bonding of MnC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro bioimaging, cytotoxicity, and cell tracking study
ce:section.ce:para: The major objective of the current study is to develop a simple method for a dual mode bio-imaging probe using MnCs. In vitro bioimaging was conducted using MSCs growing upon poly-l-lysine coated coverslips (Fig. 4 a, b, c). From the above figure, the MSCs can be imaged using a fluorescence microscope by using MnCs as a nanoprobe. The particles entered the cellular cytoplasm. However, a clear dark spot near nucleus showed MnCs did not enter the nucleus. Moreover, no overlap was seen in between DAPI stained area and MnCs which clearly confers to the above claim. Therefore, we can suggest a selective cytoplasmic entry of MnCs with nominal presence of the nanoparticle in the nuclear compartment.

The cellular applicability was checked via MTT assay (Fig. 4d) in both short term and long term. MnCs in short-term (1d) and (3d) showed highly bioactive nature in cell growth and viability was observed to be more than control. However, there is a declination in viability after 7d. This could be attributed to confluence and beginning of death kinetics to the samples which were already showing higher cell growth in 3d due to contact inhibition. The MnCs could be considered as cytocompatible.

Further, in vitro cell tracking study was continued for up to 9 days (4 passages/12 generations) to observe cell tracking capability of MnCs. While for first few generations the cells were bright enough to get clear fluorescence images even from the long depth of focus from the flask (up to day 5), beyond passage 3 the signal was observed to diminish, and it was barely visible beyond passage 4 (Fig. 5 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA quantification and in vitro ROS scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular activity was measured in terms of DNA quantification of the cells (Fig. 6a). It has been observed that DNA productions in samples are more than control. It followed the similar pattern of MTT assay. Moreover, there is a concentration-dependent increase in the DNA production in a linear mode.

The major concern for ROS generation in vivo models is a limitation of many of the MR imaging probes like Gd-chelates. Therefore, DCFH-DA assay and NBT assay (Fig. 6b, c) were conducted to find out if MnCs can scavenge ROS from the cellularmicroenvironment. In the case of DCFH-DA assay, the result was however not satisfactory. In higher dosage of MnCs, the response was higher than control. However, in the case of NBT assay, the results were observed to be positive. The results were observed to be statistically significant in all the concentrations. Hence, it could be inferred that MnCs are better in scavenging superoxide rather than hydroxyl radicals.

In vivo application of MR imaging requires infusion/injection of MnCs intravenously. However, this requires the material to be hemocompatible. For that hemolysis and blood cell aggregation assay were conducted. In hemolysis assay (Fig. 7a), all the concentrations of the sample showed negligible hemolysis with respect to Triton-X. All of them showed lower hemolysis than 5%. Even though there was certain aggregation possibility observed in RBCs, nominal aggregation was observed in WBCs and platelets (Fig. 7b, c,and d). Therefore, the possibility of immune action or blood clot formation could be negligible. Some small aggregation in WBCs was observed, majority of cells were observed not to be aggregation free. So, the material could be considered blood compatible.

The RT-PCR study of the material was conducted to check whether it can down-regulate the genes responsible in the body for ROS scavenging. The genes concerned evaluated in this study are Glutathione Transferase (GT), NAD-Oxidoreductase (NAD) and Superoxide Dismutase (SOD) while GAPDH (GAP) is kept as housekeeping gene. The results were reported in Fig. 8 . While two secondary ROS scavenging enzymes were over-expressed in MnCs and lower in control, huge difference in SOD expression was observed which was also statistically significant. This down-regulation of SOD also matches with the NBT results of the sample. GT is involved in hydroxyl radical scavenging whose expression pattern was like DCFH-DA assay.

The MR imaging was performed to check its applicability as a contrast agent (Fig. 9 ). It has been observed that in T1 there is a concentration-dependent increase in intensity. A strong T1 signal was obtained at a low concentration of 20 μg/mL. There was an increase in intensity when the concentration was further increased from 40 to 100 μg/mL The T1 values were calculated based on inversion-recovery fast spin-echo sequence with variable TI. The longitudinal relaxivity (r1) of MnC was obtained from the slope of the linear fitted relaxivity plot shown in Fig. 9B. The increasing nature of the relaxivity plot corresponds to the enhanced relaxivity of MnC and the estimated r1 value is 3.98 mM−1.sec−1 at 1.5 T which indicates paramagnetic nature of MnC and most of the commercially available T1 contrast agents have the r1 values in the same range [21]. Therefore, the material has comparable performance with respect to standard contrast agents and it could be useful for a T1 contrast agent in MR imaging.

A similar observation was reflected in the animal MRI. Fig. 10 shows the T1 weighted MR images of mice before injection and after injection of MnCs at two different time intervals. At 10 min post-injection, a significant contrast enhancement was observed. The brightening effect was prominent at 30 min where all the organs and anatomical positions are clear. This observation clearly indicates the T1 relaxation effect of injected MnCs in mice. Furthermore, MR images (Fig. 10) at 30 min shows enhanced signal intensity in the kidney which indicates the accumulation of the magnetic material in this organ for the gradual removal from the body through renal clearance. This renal clearance is a positive indication of biocompatibility of synthesized MnCs.

The histology of liver and kidney of the rodent model which was undergone MR imaging was performed to understand the hepatotoxicity and renal toxicity of MnCs as they are the major clearance routes of the body (Fig. 11 ). As observed from the MR imaging also, the major signal of MnCs was observed to be intensified in kidney depicting that to be major clearance route.

The kidney structure was observed to be normal with no specific traces of toxicity. No specific effect of MnCs accumulation, inflammation, rupture of renal tubule or fluid accumulation was observed suggesting in vivo renal safety while applying MnCs for imaging. A similar trend of nontoxic histological features like no significant inflammation, granulation or edema was observed in case of the section of hepatic tissue section also. Thus, MnCs are observed to be safe and nonaccumulative in the body with minimal toxicity.

KMnO4 mediated carbon dots substituted MnO2nanostructure development is a novel chemical route with very few recent reports of super-capacitors and energy storage applications. [24–26] While this application of the material was recent, similar materials synthesized from different precursors were even more recent for the literature to be applied for medicinal purpose [27,28]. However, most the biomedical reports do not go into the cellular level to evaluate the safety of the materials to be administered in the in vivo system. The major T1 contrast agent explored for MR imaging over last three decades are gadolinium oxides (Gd) which recently has been observed to have multiple toxicity issues majorly due to its accumulation and non-clearance issues to the major excretory organs of the living systems such as liver and kidney. The major clearance route of any contrast from the human body is liver. However, Gd was observed to cause severe hepatic toxicity even to the level of liver cirrhosis on chronic exposure [29,30]. In the current study, however, we observed no significant toxicity in the liver suggesting its applicability (Fig. 10b). However, the major clearance route for the MnCs was observed to be via kidneys with a significant change in contrast (Fig. 9a–c). MnC particles also have significant relaxivity (r1) with respect to commercially available contrast agents. While Gd agents were observed to have severe renal toxicity issue, MnCs were observed to have no significant toxicity towards the renal system (Fig. 10a) [31]. Even the in vitro study also showed not only at the cellular level (Fig. 6) MnCs could scavenge ROS but also there is significant downregulation of ROS scavenging enzyme genes (Fig. 8). From the XPS and FTIR study this can be postulated that the linkage between carbon and manganese nanostructures is established via oxygen bridges. Further, the singlet electron transport takes place via surface side chains to the bulk carbon nanostructure further allowing to deplete the ROS into carbonate or bicarbonate ions which also constitute the major buffer of cellular micro environment (Fig. 12 ). The proposed mechanism of the electron transfer is also supported by previous literature [32,33].

Fluorescence and magnetic resonance imaging are complementary techniques to each other. While the first mode helps in anatomical structure imaging the other one allows functional imaging. This multimodal imaging and multiplexing allow the samples to provide significant information in cancer biology, clinical neurology, and pathophysiology. There are multiple pieces of evidence where multimodal imaging probes were observed to be better in terms of performance in biological imaging experiments [34]. Carbon substitution also observed to generate dual mode imaging in Gadolinia molecules [35]. However, the current study involves in situ scavenging of reactive oxygen species which might help in long-term cell tracking via reducing phototoxicity (Figs. 5 and 6). Moreover, there isevidence where manganese oxides were observed to generate neurotoxicity [17]. The above results suggest MnCs can be significantly safe in terms of cellular toxicity and have tendencies towards major clearance from the body via renal route. The histology of kidney showed no significant toxicity. Therefore, MnC can be considered as a smart nanoprobe for long-term multimodal imaging with therapeutic properties and inbuilt safety features.

The current study shows a microwave assisted novel rapid synthesis of carbon dot substituted manganese oxide nanoparticles synthesis. The synthesized material (MnCs) is biocompatible, blood compatible and effective for fluorescence as well as MR imaging. Importantly, the material possesses inherent ROS scavenging property and inclination towards renal route for clearance from the biological system. Thus, MnCs mitigate the imaging related problems of cytotoxicity, free radicals and phototoxicity by self-scavenging. The probe is capable for cell tracking up to 4 passages in vitro and comparable to standard MR contrast probes in terms of molecular relaxivity and MR contrast in vivo. The material can be therefore explored further for cell delivery and large animal study as a smart dual mode nanoprobe with in situ built in safety features for future commercialization.
The authors would like to acknowledge MHRD and CSIR (Govt of India) for the fellowship of Bodhisatwa Das and Pallabi Pal respectively.
